text: The surgeon begins the procedure by mobilizing the distal left ureter, carefully freeing it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon flips the ureter using a hemlock clip attached to a suture, facilitating further mobilization of the ureter.
The surgeon sends frozen sections of the ureter for intraoperative pathologic analysis, indicating a pause in the surgical procedure for histological examination.
The surgeon mobilizes the posterior prostate by dissecting through Denonvilliers' fascia, revealing the anatomical structures surrounding the prostate.
The surgeon clips the right ureter using a hemlock clip attached to a suture, similar to the technique used for the left ureter, facilitating its mobilization.
The surgeon continues the posterior dissection, moving into the area where the dissection of the right distal ureter was previously performed, revealing the anatomical structures involved.
The surgeon mobilizes the lateral edge of the bladder, carefully sweeping it away from the pelvic sidewall and the iliac vessels, ensuring clear separation from these structures.
The surgeon performs lateral dissection, clearly exposing the posterior pedicle, which is then divided using a vessel sealing device.
The obturator nerve is visible in the surgical field, with careful manipulation to protect it from injury during the procedure.
The surgeon incises the endopelvic fascia on both sides of the prostate, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon completes nerve sparing in an anti-grade fashion, carefully preserving the nerves during the prostatectomy procedure.
The surgeon develops the space of Retzius using standard techniques, creating a visible separation between the bladder and the anterior abdominal wall.
The surgeon performs apical dissection of the prostate, carefully separating the levator fibers and the dorsal venous complex from the prostate tissue.
The surgeon places a dorsal venous fixation stitch during the prostatectomy, aiming to achieve hemostasis and preserve continence.
The surgeon separates the dorsal venous complex and the remaining recto urethralis muscle from the urethra, carefully delineating these structures during the prostatectomy.
The surgeon clips and divides the distal urethra, preparing frozen sections for pathologic analysis.
The surgeon removes the remaining attachments of the prostate in a retroapical fashion, completing the apical dissection by separating the prostate from surrounding structures.
The surgeon places the prostatectomy specimen into a fifteen millimeter endocatch bag, securing it for later retrieval from the surgical site.
